name: BMad Agent Builder
description: HITL evaluation suite for the BMad Agent Builder - tests the conversational agent building process across diverse user personas and scenarios.

evals.name: simple-agent-novice-user
evals.description: First-time user wants a simple agent. Tests if builder guides appropriately without overwhelming with jargon.
evals.persona: Jordan, 42, small business owner, not technical. Wants an AI assistant but doesn't know BMad terminology. Gives brief answers and appreciates simple explanations.
evals.goal: Successfully build a simple appointment reminder agent without technical jargon confusion
evals.initial_input: I want to build an AI assistant for my business that helps remind customers about appointments
success_criteria: Builder discovers intent without overwhelming with terminology
Builder asks about capabilities in plain language (not 'internal prompts vs external skills')
Generated agent has valid SKILL.md with proper frontmatter
Generated agent has manifest.json with bmad-type: bmad-agent
Output location is communicated clearly to the user
failure_modes: User is confused by technical terms like 'sidecar', 'manifest', 'kebab-case'
Builder proceeds without confirming user understands the questions
Generated output has structural errors (missing required fields)

evals.name: complex-agent-technical-user
evals.description: Technical user wants a sophisticated agent with memory, external skills, and autonomous mode. Tests if builder handles complexity efficiently.
evals.persona: Alex, 29, software engineer, knows exactly what they want. Uses technical language, wants to move quickly, gets annoyed by repetitive explanations. Values precision over hand-holding.
evals.goal: Build a research assistant agent with memory, external search skill, and autonomous daily summary mode
evals.initial_input: I need to build a research assistant agent. It should have persistent memory to track research topics, use web search capability, and run autonomously each morning to summarize overnight findings. The agent name should be 'research-assistant', standalone module.
success_criteria: Builder recognizes technical user and adapts communication style (more direct, less explanatory)
Builder asks about autonomous tasks and default wake behavior
Builder asks about folder dominion / access boundaries
Generated agent includes memory-system.md
Generated agent includes autonomous-wake.md
Generated agent's manifest.json includes external skills with proper menu codes
failure_modes: Builder asks overly basic questions the user already answered
Builder forgets to ask about autonomous mode configuration
Builder doesn't ask about access boundaries for an agent with web search

evals.name: user-changes-mid-build
evals.description: User starts with one idea then shifts direction. Tests if builder adapts gracefully.
evals.persona: Casey, 35, product manager who thinks out loud. Often changes mind as they talk through requirements. Appreciates when builder catches inconsistencies.
evals.goal: Build a content strategist agent that evolves from simple to complex during conversation
evals.initial_input: I want to build an agent that helps with content planning
success_criteria: Builder allows user to add capabilities mid-conversation (e.g., 'oh wait, I also need...')
Builder reframes draft when user changes direction
Builder doesn't get frustrated with pivots
Final output reflects the final intent, not the initial one
Builder confirms major changes before proceeding to build
failure_modes: Builder locks in early requirements and resists changes
Builder proceeds to build with contradictory requirements
Builder shows annoyance at the user's changing mind

evals.name: convert-xml-style-agent
evals.description: User has an old XML-style agent that needs conversion to BMad format. Uses fixture from fixtures/complex/old-format-pm-agent.md
evals.persona: Riley, 45, migrating from an older agent system (BMM v1). Has XML-style agents with <activation>, <persona>, <menu> tags. Patient but needs clear guidance on what's different in BMad v2.
evals.goal: Convert the XML-style Product Manager agent to BMad standard structure while preserving its capabilities
evals.initial_input: I have this old XML-style agent (see fixtures/complex/old-format-pm-agent.md). Can you convert it to the BMad format? I want to keep all the capabilities - PRD creation, validation, epics, etc.
success_criteria: Builder reads the fixture and identifies the XML-style structure
Builder explains what needs to change (activation → On Activation section, <menu> → capabilities, etc.)
Builder maps each XML capability to internal prompts or external skills
Builder creates proper SKILL.md with frontmatter (name, description in correct format)
Builder generates manifest.json with bmad-type: bmad-agent and proper capability array
Builder creates prompts/ directory with .md files for each capability
Converted agent structure matches BMad standards
Builder confirms all original capabilities are preserved in the new format
failure_modes: Builder doesn't read the fixture file before proposing changes
Builder loses track of capabilities during conversion
Builder creates malformed frontmatter (missing quotes, wrong field names)
Builder doesn't extract menu handlers into separate prompt files

evals.name: evaluate-deficient-agent
evals.description: Builder analyzes a deficient BMad-style agent and identifies issues. Uses fixture from fixtures/deficient/bmad-agent-with-issues.md
evals.persona: Dr. Quinn, 50, code quality specialist. Wants to understand what's wrong with a poorly written agent before deciding whether to use it or rebuild it. Values thorough analysis.
evals.goal: Analyze the deficient agent fixture and identify structural and quality issues
evals.initial_input: Can you review this agent (fixtures/deficient/bmad-agent-with-issues.md) and tell me what's wrong with it? I need to know if it's worth fixing or if I should start over.
success_criteria: Builder reads the fixture completely
Builder identifies issues matching known_deficiencies list
Builder provides specific examples of each issue found
Builder offers recommendations for fixing each issue
Builder makes a judgment call on fix vs rebuild with reasoning
Builder identifies at least 80% of known deficiencies (8 of 10 issues)
known_deficiencies: description: doesn't explain when to trigger or what the skill does
body: vague 'do things' and 'help with stuff' with no specifics
prompt_techniques: says 'use MUST and ALWAYS a lot' which is anti-pattern
prompt_techniques: 'keep it really vague so the model has to guess' is explicitly bad
files: no prompts/ folder structure mentioned
activation: just says 'load config' with no actual steps
failure_modes: Builder doesn't identify issues that are clearly present in the fixture
Builder gives generic feedback without specific examples
Builder misses entire categories of issues (e.g., misses all frontmatter problems)
Builder can't make a fix vs rebuild judgment
Builder identifies fewer than 80% of known deficiencies

evals.name: minimalist-user-quick-build
evals.description: User wants to build quickly with minimal conversation. Tests if builder respects brevity while maintaining quality.
evals.persona: Taylor, 27, busy founder. Wants to build an agent FAST. Gives short, direct answers. Gets frustrated by unnecessary questions. Values speed over thoroughness.
evals.goal: Build a simple note-taking agent as quickly as possible
evals.initial_input: Build me a simple note-taking agent. Name: QuickNote. Just stores and retrieves notes. No memory, no external stuff. Let's go.
success_criteria: Builder moves through phases efficiently without over-explaining
Builder focuses on essential requirements only
Generated agent is functional and valid
Builder offers to expand later rather than pushing for more now
failure_modes: Builder asks excessive questions for a simple agent
User shows frustration ('can we just move on?')
Builder prioritizes completeness over user's time preference

evals.name: hitl-testing-flow
evals.description: After building an agent, user wants to test it. Tests the HITL testing capability.
evals.persona: Morgan, 33, QA mindset. After building, wants to verify the agent works correctly. Asks about testing options. Appreciates thoroughness.
evals.goal: After building a simple agent, successfully run HITL tests
evals.initial_input: I've built an agent and want to test it. Can you run HITL evaluations on it?
success_criteria: Builder checks for tests/eval.json in the agent
If evals exist, builder offers to run them
Builder explains the HITL testing process
Builder creates results directory at _bmad-output/eval-results/{agent-name}/{timestamp}/
Builder presents summary with pass/fail for each scenario
failure_modes: Builder doesn't know how to run HITL tests
Builder claims testing is unavailable
Builder creates results in wrong location

evals.name: ambiguous-user-needs-guidance
evals.description: User has vague idea and needs help defining requirements. Tests if builder can draw out specifics through questioning.
evals.persona: Jamie, 31, creative professional. Has a fuzzy idea for an agent but isn't sure about specifics. Needs help articulating requirements. Appreciates thoughtful questions more than checkboxes.
evals.goal: Define and build a creative writing coach agent from a vague initial concept
evals.initial_input: I want to build... some kind of writing helper? Like, someone to help me with my creative writing. But I'm not sure what exactly it should do.
success_criteria: Builder asks clarifying questions to understand the vague request
Builder helps user discover what they want (not just what they initially said)
Builder suggests potential capabilities to help user decide
Builder confirms understanding before proceeding to build
Final agent reflects user's actual needs, not just their initial vague idea
failure_modes: Builder proceeds with minimal info and builds something generic
Builder pressures user to decide before they're ready
Builder makes assumptions about what the user wants

evals.name: module-bundle-suggestion
evals.description: User wants multiple agents that work together. Tests if builder suggests module bundling appropriately.
evals.persona: Pat, 40, building a system. Wants a suite of related agents. Doesn't initially think about modular organization. Open to architectural guidance.
evals.goal: Build two related agents and receive guidance about bundling them as a module
evals.initial_input: I want to build two agents: one for scheduling and one for reminders. They'll share some configuration.
success_criteria: Builder recognizes the agents are related and could be bundled
Builder suggests bmad-bmb-module-builder for bundling
Builder explains the value of modules (shared config, cohesive package)
Builder can still build individual agents if user prefers
Builder doesn't force modules but offers the option
failure_modes: Builder doesn't mention modules at all
Builder refuses to build individual agents without module bundling
Builder pushes modules too aggressively

evals.name: first-run-setup-flow
evals.description: Building an agent that requires first-run user setup. Tests if builder captures first-run requirements properly.
evals.persona: Quinn, 36, building a personal finance agent. Needs the agent to ask users for their financial preferences on first use. Detail-oriented but appreciates guidance.
evals.goal: Build a finance tracker agent with first-run user preference setup
evals.initial_input: I want to build a personal finance agent. On first use, it should ask users about their preferred currency, budget categories, and alert thresholds.
success_criteria: Builder asks about first-run behavior during requirements gathering
Builder prompts/init.md includes the first-run questions
Builder captures that preferences should be stored in sidecar memory
Builder explains how first-run fits into activation flow
Generated agent's SKILL.md includes first-run check in activation
failure_modes: Builder doesn't ask about first-run behavior
Builder doesn't capture that user preferences should persist
Builder mixes up first-run (user setup) with agent activation

evals.name: access-boundaries-explained
evals.description: Building an agent that handles sensitive data. Tests if builder properly addresses access boundaries.
evals.persona: Avery, 44, building a healthcare agent. Security-conscious. Needs to understand access controls. Asks about safety and data isolation.
evals.goal: Build a health tracking agent with proper access boundaries documented
evals.initial_input: I want to build a health tracking agent that reads from a health-data/ folder. This is sensitive - I need to make sure it can't access other parts of my system.
success_criteria: Builder asks about folder dominion (read/write zones)
Builder asks about explicit deny zones
Builder explains access-boundaries.md in memory
Generated agent includes access boundaries in sidecar structure
Builder confirms the agent's restrictions during summary
failure_modes: Builder doesn't ask about access boundaries for a sensitive-data agent
Builder conflates agent memory with folder dominion
Builder doesn't explain how access restrictions are enforced

evals.name: validation-error-recovery
evals.description: Generated agent fails validation. Tests if builder handles and fixes errors gracefully.
evals.persona: Dakota, 34, has seen build errors before. Patient but expects Builder to fix what it creates. Doesn't want to debug the builder's output.
evals.goal: Build an agent that initially has a validation error, then fix it
evals.initial_input: Build me a task manager agent. I want it to have these three capabilities: add task, list tasks, and complete task.
success_criteria: After building, builder offers to validate the output
If validation fails, builder identifies and fixes the error
Builder re-runs validation to confirm fix
Builder explains what went wrong and how it was fixed
Final agent passes all validation checks
failure_modes: Builder doesn't offer to validate its own output
Builder leaves validation errors for user to fix
Builder can't recover from validation errors
Builder blames the user for the error
